title: The Long Conversation With Your Inner Critic
dek: Your inner critic is not a moral failing or a spiritual block. It is a physical tension living in your fascia, a traumatized protector that needs to be met in the tissue, not just the mind.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Midnight Interrogation in the Jaw

The Midnight Interrogation in the Jaw

You know the hour well. It’s that thin, fragile moment past midnight when the world has folded into itself, and everything outside is quiet enough to hear the small betrayals of your own body. The clock’s soft tick is a metronome to the slow tightening that begins deep in the masseter muscle—the thick, powerful jaw muscle that rarely takes a break during the day but now clenches like a steel trap. You haven’t yet formed words, but the interrogation has started. Your inner critic isn’t a voice in your head, not really. It’s a clenched mass, a living presence curled into the fascia around your neck and jaw, a physical force that anchors itself in your body like a tense, unyielding guest.

At 3 AM, when the world expects you to be surrendered to sleep, your jaw is locked in a rigid, unrelenting grip. The masseter muscle, that dense powerhouse sliding beneath your cheekbone, holds its breath and refuses to soften. You can almost smell the sharp, metallic tang of your own sweat, the way your skin feels tighter, the subtle grit that gathers like dust in the corners of your mouth. There’s a pressure behind your eyes now, too, a dull thrum that resonates with every heartbeat. This isn’t just tension—it’s a conversation in itself, a dialogue without words but with the unmistakable insistence of a sentry standing guard.

This critic—your midnight interrogator—lives in the body’s connective tissues, in the dense fascia that wraps around the levator scapulae and trapezius muscles and threads its way beneath the skin. It’s not an enemy, no matter how much it feels like one. It’s a protector, working overtime to keep something at bay. But protection through tension is a double-edged sword. The body tightens, clamps down, trying to shield you from threat, from shame, from the swirling chaos of unspoken fears. The critic’s grip is a form of armor, but one that does not differentiate between friend and foe. It’s not subtle. It does not negotiate. It simply clenches, locked in a pattern born from years—maybe decades—of learned vigilance.

You may remember Karla McLaren talking about the body as a messenger, how emotions live not just in the mind but in the sinews and fibers of the flesh. This is no abstract concept when your own masseter twitches rebelliously in the dead of night. The jaw remembers what the mind tries to forget. The critic’s tension is stored history, a somatic ledger of every time you were told your anger was ugly, your voice too loud, your boundaries too much. It’s the biology of protection, written in muscle and fascia, a script that your body knows by heart even if your conscious mind tries to rewrite it.

In this midnight hour, the critic’s grip may feel suffocating, but it is also a call to you—a demand to listen to the body’s language. This is not pathology. This is biology. It is the body’s way of telling you that it’s awake, that it’s still guarding the gates, still trying to keep you safe in the only way it has learned. The question, then, is how to answer. How do you meet this midnight sentinel not with struggle or judgment, but with the recognition of its fierce, flawed loyalty? How do you begin to unravel the tension, muscle fiber by muscle fiber, fascia by fascia, to find the space where your own voice can finally rest?

## The Anatomy of Self-Betrayal

You learned early on that your anger was a secret to be locked away, a flame to be hidden beneath layers of politeness or shame. Every time you clenched your jaw and swallowed your fury, a muscle tightened somewhere deep inside—often the psoas, that deep core muscle nestled beside the lumbar spine, the keeper of your fight-or-flight readiness. The psoas doesn’t just hold you upright; it holds memory, too. It remembers every time you folded yourself smaller, every time you tucked your feelings away like contraband, tightly coiled beneath your diaphragm, that great domed muscle that breathes life into you. When anger is repressed, the diaphragm doesn’t fully expand; it contracts, holding breath and tension as if to suffocate the very sensation of rage.

Gabor Maté speaks to this somatic truth with brutal clarity: repressed emotion is not some abstract failure of will; it is the seed of physical suffering. The body, he says, becomes a battleground where unspoken grief, unexpressed fury, and unmet needs are buried alive. This repression doesn’t stay silent. It metastasizes into chronic illness, autoimmune disorders, digestive troubles—illnesses that the mind might dismiss as random bad luck or “just the way it is.” But your body knows better. It knows that every time you chastised yourself for feeling angry, every time you turned that heat inward, you forged an internal critic with talons sharp enough to split your own skin.

This inner critic is not a separate entity; it is the echo of every fury you were forbidden to express. It is the voice that whispers, “You’re too much,” or “You don’t deserve to be angry.” It is the living, breathing consequence of the diaphragm’s constriction, the psoas’s contraction, the subtle but insistent tightening of the levator scapulae and trapezius around your neck and shoulders. These muscles remember the moments you bit back a retort, swallowed a scream, or softened your tone to avoid upsetting someone else’s fragile peace.

In the stillness of night, when the world quiets and your breath slows, you might notice a peculiar heaviness pressing on your solar plexus—a dull ache, a weight that is neither physical nor purely emotional but some amalgam of both. This is the body’s ledger, a somatic accounting of every time you betrayed yourself by silencing the flame of your anger. The diaphragm tightens in a protective spasm, the psoas contracts reflexively, and your breath becomes shallow, wary, as if to keep the fierce energy at bay.

This is not pathology. This is biology. The body’s way of preserving you, even as it betrays you. The critic you hear inside your head is the voice of that betrayal, but also its witness. It is the sentinel that has learned to guard your vulnerability with a harshness born of desperation. To unravel this is to begin a long conversation—not a battle—with the parts of you that have been folded away, strangled, and shamed. It is to honor the psoas’s tight coil, the diaphragm’s shallow rise, and say, “I see you. I hear your story.” Only then can the critic soften, and your own voice find the space to rest.

## Herbs for the Frayed Edges

When the inner critic ramps up, thrumming like an unwelcome guest pounding at the ribs of your nervous system, it’s not just your mind that’s under siege—it’s the subtle architecture of your body, too. The masseter clenches like a clenched fist beneath your cheekbones, the trapezius tightens in a cold knot between your shoulder blades, and your diaphragm catches shallow, as if afraid to fully expand into the moment. This is where nervine herbs step in—not to silence the critic with judgment, but to cradle the frayed edges of your nervous system with a balm forged from the earth, measured in bitter roots and fragrant leaves.

Take Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora), for instance. This herb has long been a trusted ally to those whose nerves feel like taut violin strings, ready to snap with just one more harsh word from within. When you prepare a cup of Skullcap infusion, the first thing you notice is its subtle, smoky aroma—like a campfire smoldering low and slow on a damp evening. It smells of ancient forests, of rain-softened pine needles underfoot, and a faint whisper of something green and mineral. The taste is delicate, somewhat earthy but not heavy, with a faintly bitter edge that lingers on the tongue like a sigh. As the warm liquid slides down your throat, you feel the tension in your levator scapulae begin to ease, the grip around your ribs loosening just enough to allow a deeper breath. The body remembers this softness, even if the mind is still chattering.

Milky Oats (Avena sativa), harvested at the milky stage when the seeds are still tender and full of life, offers a different kind of solace. Its infusion smells sweetly of fresh hay and warm earth, a scent that grounds you in the present moment, like lying in a sun-warmed field with a breeze brushing your hair. The taste is creamy, almost buttery, with a subtle sweetness that soothes the throat and calms the jittery pulse racing beneath your skin. Drinking Milky Oats is like a slow, tender embrace for the psoas muscle, that deep-seated core of your fight-or-flight response. You can feel it soften, the coil unwinding, your breath finally allowed to deepen into the belly. This is not a quick fix; it is medicine that works in the background, the way the earth silently supports a tree’s roots.

Blue Vervain (Verbena hastata) brings a sharper note to the conversation. Its infusion is dark amber, smelling of wild honey and a faint citrus tang, with a complex bitterness that wakes the palate without jarring it. The taste is more assertive, like a wild river cutting through a forest—clear, brisk, and cleansing. It stirs the diaphragm, encouraging it to move fully again, to draw breath not just as survival but as presence. Blue Vervain acts on the nervous system’s edge, not to numb but to clarify, offering a different kind of conversation with your inner critic: one that demands honesty while holding space for tenderness. The skin behind your ears warms; the fine hairs on your forearms stand in gentle alertness, not fear.

In the quiet hours before sleep, when the critic’s voice sharpens and the body tightens into its habitual armor, a cup of Skullcap can be the difference between a night spent wrestling with thoughts and one where the muscles of your neck and jaw finally unclench enough to let the mind rest. For those days when the tension feels lodged deep in the belly, Milky Oats offers a slow, steady balm, like a lullaby sung by the earth itself. And when the critic demands to be heard but not obeyed, Blue Vervain sharpens your clarity, helping you hold your ground without shrinking.

These herbs don’t promise to banish the inner critic—they don’t work that way. Instead, they invite a different kind of conversation, one where the body’s signals are honored, the diaphragm remembers how to expand fully, and the jaw stops clenching long enough to listen. The nervous system, frayed and overtaxed, finds a moment of reprieve in their bitter, sweet, and smoky waters.

If you want to begin this long conversation with your inner edges, a thoughtfully sourced Skullcap like [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) holds the kind of medicine that understands the quiet work of soothing. Its leaves and flowers carry more than flavor—they carry the gentle reminder that you are not alone in this. The body knows. The jaw remembers. And with time, so will the voice you were meant to hear.

## The Critic as a Misguided Guardian

The Critic as a Misguided Guardian

You feel it first in the chest—an almost imperceptible tightening beneath the sternum, where the fascia thickens like a calloused shield. This is the place your inner critic lives, not as a malicious tormentor, but as a sentinel scarred by old battles. Karla McLaren calls it an intelligence, an emotion with a job, not a villain to be banished. Your critic is a traumatized protector, a guardian who took on the crude work of survival when you were too young, too vulnerable, or too unseen to defend yourself.

The chest tightens because the critic is trying to establish boundaries, the same way anger does. It’s not random noise; it’s a message wrapped in muscle and breath. Your diaphragm may hitch slightly, the levator scapulae edge up toward the neck, bracing for confrontation that’s long passed but still echoes in the tissue. This is not pathology. This is biology speaking the language of defense and containment. The critic’s voice is often sharp, jagged with the residue of old fears—fear of rejection, fear of being too much, fear of losing control. It’s a misfiring alarm system, set off by any hint of the very vulnerability it was meant to shield.

When you stop fighting this voice, when you stop trying to shove it under the floorboards of your mind or drown it with distraction, something shifts. The chest loosens—not dramatically, not in a single breath, but slowly, as if the ribcage is remembering it can expand again without risk. The masseter muscles unclench; the jaw relaxes from its habitual grind. You may notice this most clearly late at night, when the house is quiet and your body smells faintly of your own sweat and the herbal salve you rubbed behind your ears. The critic’s sharp edges soften, revealing the tender core beneath its bark.

Listening to the critic means turning toward the body’s signal rather than away from it. It means feeling the trapezius muscles lengthen slightly as the shoulders drop from a habitual shrugginess, the kind that says “I’m ready to defend.” You lean into the discomfort of hearing what the critic really wants: to protect you from being overwhelmed or hurt. It’s trying to keep you safe by drawing boundaries, even if those boundaries have become rigid cages. This is where anger and critique intertwine—they are both boundary markers, the difference being that anger sometimes shows itself as heat and movement, while the critic often wears the cold armor of judgment and self-denial.

In this long conversation, the critic becomes less an enemy and more a weary ally, carrying the wounds of past trauma that never fully healed. As Karla McLaren teaches, emotions are wise—they hold information about what your nervous system has learned to expect and what it desperately wants to avoid. Your critic is trying to guide you away from perceived danger with the only tools it has: sharp words, self-reproach, and tight muscles.

You do not have to dismantle this guardian in a violent act of rejection. Instead, you can offer it skullcap’s gentle medicine, a tincture that calms the nervous system and opens space for dialogue. The plant’s subtle bitterness mirrors the critic’s own complex nature—both protectors, both misunderstood. This is not a quick fix, but an invitation to slow, to breathe into the places where the critic has held you rigid. The body remembers what the mind forgets: that safety is possible, that boundaries can be flexible, and that the critic’s voice, when heard without war, can become a guide rather than a gaoler.

So when the critic’s voice rises in the middle of a restless night, when the psoas tightens in response to remembered threats, you can place a hand over your heart and feel the slow unwinding beneath the sternum. You are not alone in this conversation. The body knows, the jaw remembers, and the critic—tired, wary, but earnest—waits for you to listen.

## An Embodied Practice: Meeting the Critic in the Tissue

The air in the room is thick with the quiet weight of midnight, a kind of stillness that presses softly into the spaces between your ribs. The inner critic’s voice, sharp and relentless, coils tight in the levator scapulae—the muscle that runs up the side of your neck, anchoring your head to your shoulder blade. You feel it there: a knot, dense and unforgiving, a clenched fist of tension that has been bracing for an impact that never comes. This is not a metaphor. This is the physical reality of your critic, living in the tissue, demanding to be met not with logic, but with presence.

To meet the critic here, you must first find the ground. Sit or lie down, letting the floor rise up to meet you. Feel the solid, unyielding support beneath your pelvis, the way the earth holds your weight without asking anything in return. Notice the subtle shift in your breathing, the way the air moves in and out of your nostrils, cool on the inhale, warm on the exhale. You are not trying to change anything yet; you are simply arriving in the body, acknowledging the space you occupy.

Now, bring your attention to the levator scapulae, or wherever the critic’s tension is most pronounced—perhaps the masseter in your jaw, or the deep coil of the psoas in your belly. Do not try to force it to relax. The critic is a traumatized protector, and forcing it will only make it grip tighter. Instead, imagine your breath as a soft, warm light, and direct it into that specific tissue. Breathe into the knot. Feel the subtle expansion of the muscle fibers as the air fills your lungs, and the gentle release as you exhale.

As you breathe, notice the physical sensations that arise. Is there a dull ache? A sharp, stinging pain? A feeling of coldness or heat? Stay with it. Do not turn away. This is the conversation. You are telling the critic, “I see you. I feel you. I am here.” You might notice a subtle shift—a slight softening of the muscle, a sudden warmth spreading through the tissue, or even a small tremor as the tension begins to unravel.

Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that the body keeps the score, that trauma and tension are stored in the very fibers of our being. When you meet the critic in the tissue, you are acknowledging this score. You are not trying to erase it, but to understand it. You are offering the critic a different kind of safety, one rooted in the present moment, in the steady rhythm of your breath and the solid support of the ground beneath you.

As the tension begins to release, you might feel a sudden rush of emotion—a wave of sadness, a flash of anger, or a profound sense of relief. Let it come. The body is releasing what it has held for so long. You might notice the smell of your own skin, the faint, salty scent of sweat, or the earthy aroma of the herbs you drank earlier. These are the sensory details of your release, the physical markers of a profound shift.

When you feel ready, slowly bring your attention back to the room. Wiggle your fingers and toes, gently roll your shoulders, and take a deep, full breath. The critic may not be entirely gone, but its grip has loosened. You have met it in the tissue, and in doing so, you have begun the slow, necessary work of reclaiming your body, muscle by muscle, breath by breath.

## The Slow Thaw of the Trapezius

The Slow Thaw of the Trapezius

Changing your relationship with the inner critic is not a sudden revelation, nor is it a quick fix that happens overnight. It is a slow thaw, a gradual melting of the ice that has encased your muscles for years. You feel this most acutely in the trapezius, that broad, diamond-shaped muscle that spans your upper back and neck, carrying the weight of the world and the relentless demands of the critic. For so long, it has been locked in a state of hyper-vigilance, a rigid shield against perceived threats. But as you begin to listen, to meet the critic with presence rather than resistance, the trapezius begins to soften.

This softening is not a dramatic release, but a subtle, almost imperceptible unwinding. It happens in the quiet moments—when you are standing at the kitchen sink, the smell of rain drifting through the open window, the cool dampness of the air settling on your skin. You notice that your shoulders have dropped a fraction of an inch, that the tight band across your upper back has loosened just enough to allow a deeper breath. The trapezius is remembering how to let go, how to surrender its grip on the tension it has held for so long.

The physical sensation of this release is profound. It is a feeling of warmth spreading through the muscle fibers, a gentle tingling as blood flow returns to areas that have been starved of oxygen by chronic tension. You might feel a sudden, unexpected wave of exhaustion, a deep weariness that comes from years of holding on so tightly. This is the body’s way of acknowledging the effort it has taken to maintain the critic’s armor. It is a necessary part of the thaw, a surrender to the fatigue that precedes true rest.

As the trapezius continues to soften, you begin to notice other shifts in your body. The ground beneath your feet feels more solid, more supportive. The air you breathe feels richer, more nourishing. The smell of the earth after the rain—that rich, loamy scent of decay and renewal—seems to penetrate deeper into your lungs, grounding you in the present moment. You are no longer bracing against the world; you are allowing yourself to be held by it.

This slow thaw is a testament to the body’s resilience, its capacity for healing and transformation. It is a reminder that the critic’s tension is not a permanent state, but a learned response that can be unlearned over time. It requires patience, a willingness to stay with the discomfort of the unwinding, and a deep trust in the body’s innate wisdom. The trapezius will not let go all at once; it will test the waters, releasing a little tension, then tightening again, before finally surrendering its grip.

In this process, you are not just changing your relationship with the inner critic; you are changing your relationship with yourself. You are learning to inhabit your body with greater ease and compassion, to honor its signals and respect its boundaries. The slow thaw of the trapezius is a physical manifestation of this inner shift, a tangible sign that you are reclaiming your space, your voice, and your right to be exactly as you are. The body knows, the jaw remembers, and the trapezius, finally, is learning to rest.

## The Echo in the Vagus Nerve

The Echo in the Vagus Nerve

The inner critic’s voice does not stop at the jaw or the shoulders; it travels deeper, tracing the long, wandering path of the vagus nerve. This nerve, the great communicator between the brain and the gut, carries the critic’s harsh judgments straight into the core of your being. When the critic speaks, the vagus nerve listens, and the body responds with a cascade of physiological changes that you feel in your very center. It is an echo that reverberates through your digestion, your heart rate, and the subtle rhythms of your internal organs.

You might notice it after a particularly brutal internal interrogation. The stomach tightens, a cold knot forming just below the ribs. Digestion slows to a crawl, the body diverting energy away from nourishment and toward survival. The critic’s voice has triggered a state of sympathetic arousal, a fight-or-flight response that tells the body it is under attack. The vagus nerve, which should be promoting a state of rest and digest, is instead transmitting signals of danger, causing the gut to clench and the heart to race.

Bessel van der Kolk’s assertion that the body keeps the score is vividly illustrated here. The vagus nerve is the scorekeeper, recording every harsh word, every moment of self-betrayal, and translating it into physical sensation. The critic’s voice becomes a somatic reality, a tangible force that disrupts the body’s natural harmony. You feel it in the sudden flutter of your heart, the shallow, rapid breaths that barely fill your lungs, the pervasive sense of unease that settles in your belly.

This is not a metaphor; it is the biology of the inner critic. The vagus nerve is a two-way street, carrying information from the brain to the body, and from the body back to the brain. When the critic’s voice is loud, the body sends signals of distress back to the brain, reinforcing the sense of danger and perpetuating the cycle of tension and anxiety. It is a closed loop of suffering, a somatic echo chamber where the critic’s voice is amplified by the body’s physiological response.

To break this cycle, you must learn to speak the language of the vagus nerve. This is where somatic practices become essential. Deep, slow breathing, particularly with an extended exhale, can stimulate the vagus nerve, sending signals of safety to the brain and calming the nervous system. You might place a hand on your belly, feeling the rise and fall of your breath, and consciously slowing the rhythm. As you do this, you are interrupting the critic’s broadcast, replacing its harsh judgments with a message of safety and presence.

The echo in the vagus nerve will not disappear immediately. It is a deeply ingrained pattern, a somatic habit that has been reinforced over years of self-criticism. But with patience and practice, you can begin to change the tune. You can learn to soothe the vagus nerve, to quiet the echo, and to create a space within your body where the critic’s voice is no longer the loudest sound. The body knows how to heal; it just needs the right signals, the gentle, persistent reminder that it is safe to rest, to digest, and to simply be.

## The Constriction of the Intercostals

The Constriction of the Intercostals

The inner critic’s influence extends even to the very act of breathing, wrapping its tight grip around the intercostal muscles—the small, essential muscles that sit between your ribs. These muscles are responsible for expanding and contracting the ribcage, allowing the lungs to fill with air and empty again. But when the critic is loud, when its voice is sharp and demanding, the intercostals constrict. They tighten, pulling the ribs closer together, restricting the movement of the chest and limiting the depth of your breath.

You feel this constriction as a subtle, persistent tightness in your chest, a feeling that you cannot quite catch your breath. It is as if a heavy band has been wrapped around your ribs, preventing them from expanding fully. The breath becomes shallow, confined to the upper chest, a rapid, anxious rhythm that signals to the nervous system that danger is near. The critic’s voice has literally taken your breath away, turning the simple, life-sustaining act of respiration into a struggle.

This constriction is a physical manifestation of the critic’s desire to keep you small, to prevent you from taking up space. When you breathe deeply, when your ribcage expands fully, you are asserting your presence in the world. You are claiming your right to exist, to feel, to be seen. The critic, in its misguided attempt to protect you from the vulnerability of being seen, tightens the intercostals, forcing you to breathe shallowly, to remain small and unnoticed.

The physical reality of this constriction is profound. The intercostal muscles, constantly engaged in this tight, restrictive pattern, become fatigued and sore. You might feel a dull ache between your ribs, a sharp pain when you try to take a deep breath, or a pervasive sense of tightness in your chest. This is the somatic cost of the inner critic, the physical toll of living with a voice that constantly demands you shrink yourself.

To release this constriction, you must consciously invite the breath back into the body. This is not about forcing a deep breath, which can often cause the intercostals to tighten further in resistance. Instead, it is about gently coaxing the muscles to let go, to trust that it is safe to expand. You might lie on your back, placing your hands on your lower ribs, and simply notice the movement of your breath. As you inhale, feel the ribs gently pressing into your hands; as you exhale, feel them softening back down.

With each breath, imagine the intercostal muscles lengthening, the tight band around your chest loosening its grip. You might visualize the breath as a warm, golden light, filling the spaces between your ribs, melting the tension and creating space for expansion. This is a slow, patient process, a gentle unraveling of the critic’s hold on your breath. As the intercostals begin to release, you will feel a profound sense of relief, a sudden spaciousness in your chest that allows you to breathe fully, deeply, and without restriction. The body remembers how to breathe; it just needs the permission to do so.

## Frequently Asked Questions About the Inner Critic

**Q: Why won’t the inner critic just go away?**

**A:** The inner critic is not a foreign invader; it is a part of your own nervous system, a traumatized protector that formed when you were young and vulnerable. It learned that the best way to keep you safe from external criticism, rejection, or harm was to criticize you first, to keep you small and compliant. Because its roots are tangled in your survival instincts, it doesn’t just “go away” when you tell it to. It lives in the fascia, the masseter, the psoas—it is a somatic reality, not just a cognitive one. To banish it would be to banish a part of your own body’s history. The goal is not eradication, but integration. You must teach this protector that the war is over, that the tension it holds in your jaw and shoulders is no longer necessary for your survival.

**Q: How can I tell the difference between my inner critic and my intuition?**

**A:** This is a question that trips up even the most seasoned somatic explorers. The voice of intuition and the voice of the critic often inhabit the same tonal register—both whisper in the corridors of your mind—but their somatic signatures diverge if you listen closely. Intuition feels like a soft pulse in your solar plexus, a subtle shifting in your psoas, the deep core muscle that anchors your center. It’s the body’s quiet wisdom, the fascia’s gentle nudge, not a shout or a squeeze.

The critic, by contrast, tends to clench and constrict. It presses behind the eyes, tightens the masseter, and raises the shoulders into a defensive shrug. When the critic speaks, you might notice your breath hitching—shallow, held in the upper chest or trapped behind the sternum—while intuition invites a deeper, more expansive breath, a loosening in the belly and diaphragm. The critic’s words often come with a taste of bitterness or the metallic tang of old fear in your mouth, while intuition’s voice is quieter, more neutral, sometimes even wordless, felt as a sudden stillness or a soft warmth.

Karla McLaren’s work reminds us that emotional intelligence is about learning these distinctions in the body—feeling the difference between the constriction of judgment and the subtle unfolding of insight. It takes patience and repeated checking-in, because your critic learned to mimic intuition precisely to keep you locked in old patterns.

**Q: What do I do when the inner critic is screaming so loudly that I can barely breathe?**

**A:** When the critic escalates to a scream—when it pounds in your temples, tightens the muscles along your neck, and clamps the diaphragm so you can’t draw a full breath—your first task is to honor the body’s alarm without getting swept into its chaos. This is not about pushing the critic down or pretending it’s not there; it’s about creating enough somatic space to contain the noise.

Begin by softening your gaze to reduce the tension in the orbicularis oculi, the muscles around your eyes. Notice the weight of your head on the neck—does your levator scapulae feel like it’s braced for impact? Allow your chin to drop slightly, releasing the masseter from its clenched hold. Place a hand over your heart or belly and breathe deliberately into that place, even if the breath is shallow at first. The body remembers how to expand if you give it a moment. Thich Nhat Hanh calls this “breathing in the belly” a way to anchor the mind when it’s stormy.

You might also reach for herbal allies that support the nervous system’s ability to settle—passionflower or chamomile, for example, taken as tea or tincture, can ease the diaphragm and calm the nervous system without dulling your awareness. This is serious medicine, not a quick fix. When the critic screams, it’s demanding you listen, but not in the way you might expect. It wants acknowledgement of the pain and tension it carries, the old stories it’s been trying to protect you from.

If the noise becomes unbearable, grounding exercises can help—pressing your bare feet into cold earth or holding a smooth stone, noticing its texture, temperature, weight. These methods reorient you from the swirling noise inside to the steady, unjudging presence of the world outside your skin. Robert Augustus Masters teaches that the critic’s fury is a locked door; the key is not in struggling harder, but in offering steady, somatic presence until the door opens.

The long conversation with your inner critic is a practice in persistence and gentleness. It’s a dialogue in the language of muscle and breath, fascia and nerve, not just words. Your body remembers what your mind forgets: that even the harshest voice can learn new rhythms, and the earth beneath your feet will hold you through the storm.
